title: What Is an AI Agent? A Plain-English Guide
seoTitle: What Is an AI Agent? A Plain-English Guide
metaDescription: An AI agent is an LLM in a loop: it reasons, calls tools, checks results, and repeats until the job is done. A plain-English guide for beginners.
oneLiner: Understand what turns an LLM into an agent — the loop of reasoning, tool calls, and feedback that lets a model act instead of just answer.

blocks.md: An **AI agent** is a large language model (LLM) that doesn't just answer you — it *acts*. You give it a goal, and it works toward that goal on its own: thinking about what to do next, using tools, looking at the results, and trying again until the job is finished.

Think of the difference between asking a friend *"what's the weather in Tokyo?"* and asking them *"book me the cheapest flight to Tokyo next week."* The first is one answer. The second is a small project: they have to search flights, compare prices, check dates, maybe ask you a clarifying question, then actually do the booking. An agent is built for the second kind of task.

A plain LLM is a brilliant text-completion machine. Ask it something and it produces the most likely next words — see [what is an LLM](/learn/llm-fundamentals/llm-basics/what-is-an-llm) for how that works. It has no hands. It can't check a live calendar, run code, or send an email. An agent wraps that same model in a **loop** and hands it **tools**, so the model's words can turn into real actions.
The one-line definition worth memorizing: **an agent is an LLM in a loop with tools.** Almost everything else — planning, memory, multi-agent setups — is an elaboration of those three pieces.

A chatbot can tell you *how* to reset a customer's password. An agent can actually look up the customer, reset the password, and email them the confirmation. That gap — between describing an action and performing it — is the whole point of agents, and it's why they became the dominant pattern in AI products.

Before agents, every multi-step task meant a human stitching the steps together. You'd ask the model for SQL, copy it into a database, paste the error back, ask for a fix, and repeat. Agents close that loop automatically: the model writes the query, runs it, reads the error itself, and corrects it without you in the middle. That's the same reason [coding agents](/learn/ai-coding-tools/coding-agents-assistants/what-is-claude-code) can edit a whole repository instead of handing you one snippet at a time.

items: **Developers** building anything beyond a single question-and-answer call — support bots, research assistants, data pipelines, coding tools.
**Product teams** deciding whether a feature needs a simple prompt or a full agent (often it doesn't — more on that below).
**Anyone using AI tools daily** — the coding assistant in your editor and the "deep research" button in your chat app are both agents under the hood.

blocks.md: Agents are powerful but not free. Every loop step is another LLM call, so an agent can cost and take 5–50x more than a single prompt. The skill isn't *building* agents — it's knowing when a task actually needs one.
At the center of every agent is the **agent loop** (also called the *reason–act loop*, popularized by the **ReAct** pattern). It runs the same handful of steps over and over until the goal is met:

Walk through one turn. The model **observes** the goal and everything that has happened so far. It **reasons** about what to do next — sometimes out loud, in a short chain of thought (see [chain-of-thought prompting](/learn/prompt-engineering/reasoning-techniques/chain-of-thought-prompting)). It then **acts** by either calling a tool or, if it has enough information, giving a final answer. If it called a tool, the **result** is fed back in and the loop runs again.

blocks.text: Tools are how the agent touches the world

blocks.md: A **tool** is just a function the model is allowed to call — search the web, run a SQL query, send an email, read a file. You describe each tool's name, what it does, and what inputs it needs. The model can't run code itself; instead it outputs a structured request like *"call `get_weather` with `city = Tokyo`"*, your program runs the real function, and you hand the result back. This mechanism is [function calling](/learn/llm-apis/function-calling/what-is-function-calling), and the broader pattern is [tool use](/learn/ai-agents/tool-use/what-is-tool-use).

diagram.title: One step: from words to action

blocks.md: The loop needs a **stopping condition** so it doesn't run forever. The model stops when it decides the goal is done and returns a final answer — but you also cap the maximum number of steps as a safety net, because a confused agent can otherwise loop indefinitely, burning tokens and money.

Everything the agent has seen so far — the goal, its own reasoning, every tool call and result — lives in the model's [context window](/learn/llm-fundamentals/context-windows/what-is-a-context-window). That growing transcript *is* the agent's short-term memory. When it overflows the window, you have to summarize or trim it, which is one of the central challenges of building agents.

sections.title: AI agent vs. chatbot vs. workflow

blocks.md: These three terms get blurred constantly. The cleanest way to tell them apart is **who decides what happens next**.

A **chatbot** is a single round-trip — useful and often enough. A **workflow** chains several LLM calls along a path *you* hard-coded; the model never decides the route, it just does the work at each station. An **agent** is the only one where the model itself decides the path at runtime, which makes it the most flexible *and* the least predictable of the three.

Rule of thumb from practitioners: if you can draw the steps in advance, build a **workflow**. Reach for a full **agent** only when the steps genuinely depend on what the model discovers along the way. Simpler is more reliable.

Here's a minimal agent loop in Python using the Anthropic SDK. It gives the model one tool — a calculator — and lets it loop until it produces a final answer. This is the entire core idea of agents in about 40 lines; real frameworks just add error handling, more tools, and memory management on top.

blocks.title: tiny_agent.py

blocks.md: The shape is universal across providers and frameworks: call the model, check whether it asked for a tool, run the tool, append the result, repeat. The `while True` loop *is* the agent. Notice there's no AI magic in your code — the intelligence is entirely in the model's choice of which tool to call and when to stop.

The `eval()` above is for illustration only — running model-chosen code or input is a real security risk. In production, sandbox tools, validate inputs, and watch for [prompt injection](/learn/prompt-engineering/prompt-injection-security/what-is-prompt-injection), where hidden instructions in a tool's output try to hijack the agent.

sections.title: Where agents show up in the real world

blocks.md: You're probably already using agents without naming them. A few concrete examples:

rows: Reads your repo, edits files, runs tests, fixes failures
Plans sub-questions, searches the web, reads sources, writes a report
Looks up the account, checks order status, issues a refund
Writes SQL, runs it, reads errors, charts the result

blocks.md: Two patterns power most of these. **Planning** lets an agent break a big goal into ordered sub-tasks before diving in — see [agent planning](/learn/ai-agents/planning-and-memory/what-is-agent-planning). And when one agent isn't enough, you split the work across several specialists in a [multi-agent system](/learn/ai-agents/multi-agent-computer-use/what-is-multi-agent-system) — for example a "manager" agent that delegates research to worker agents and merges their findings.

Connecting an agent to all these tools used to mean writing custom glue for every integration. The **Model Context Protocol** ([MCP](/learn/ai-agents/model-context-protocol/what-is-mcp)) standardizes that wiring, so a tool server built once works with any MCP-aware agent — think of it as a universal adapter between agents and tools.
Once the basic loop clicks, the hard parts of real agents come into focus. None of these are solved problems — they're the active frontier of agent engineering.

Agents fail in a way single prompts don't: errors **compound**. If each step is 95% reliable, a 10-step task succeeds only about 60% of the time (0.95^10). Long agent runs need guardrails — retries, validation after each tool call, and self-checks where the agent reviews its own work. This is why [LLM observability](/learn/production-llmops/observability-monitoring/what-is-llm-observability) and tracing matter so much for agents: when a 30-step run fails, you need to see exactly which step went wrong.

The transcript that serves as short-term memory keeps growing, and a bloated context both costs more and makes the model worse at finding what matters. Managing this — summarizing old steps, deciding what to keep — is its own discipline, [context engineering](/learn/prompt-engineering/context-engineering/what-is-context-engineering). For long-term memory across sessions, agents often store and retrieve facts using a [vector database](/learn/embeddings-vector-databases/vector-database-guides/what-is-a-vector-database), the same retrieval idea behind [RAG](/learn/rag/rag-fundamentals/what-is-rag).

You can write the loop by hand, as above, or use an [agent framework](/learn/agent-frameworks/choosing-a-framework/what-is-agent-framework) like LangGraph, the OpenAI Agents SDK, or the Claude Agent SDK that bundles the loop, tool plumbing, memory, and tracing. Frameworks save time but add abstraction; a widely-shared lesson from practitioners is to start with the raw loop, understand it, and adopt a framework only once you feel the pain it solves.

The more freedom an agent has, the more it can do — and the more it can go wrong. **Computer-use** agents that click around a real screen are the high-autonomy frontier, and also the highest-risk: an agent with the power to send emails or spend money needs hard limits and, often, a human approval step. Because agent behavior is non-deterministic, you can't unit-test it like normal code; instead you build [evals](/learn/evaluation-safety/evaluation-basics/what-are-llm-evals) that score whether the agent reached the goal across many runs. Evaluation, not coding, is usually the bottleneck in shipping a reliable agent.

faq.q: What is an AI agent in simple terms?
faq.a: An AI agent is a language model that works toward a goal on its own by looping: it reasons about the next step, calls a tool to take an action, reads the result, and repeats until the task is done. In short, it's an LLM in a loop with tools — it acts instead of just answering.

faq.q: What is the difference between an AI agent and a chatbot?
faq.a: A chatbot does one thing: you ask, it answers, done. An agent can take actions and run multiple steps — it can search, run code, edit files, or send an email, then check the result and keep going until the goal is reached. The agent decides its own next steps; a chatbot just responds.

faq.q: How do AI agents actually work?
faq.a: They run an agent loop. The model observes the goal and what's happened so far, reasons about what to do, then either calls a tool or gives a final answer. If it called a tool, your code runs the real function and feeds the result back, and the loop repeats until the model decides it's finished (or hits a step limit).

faq.q: Do I need to be a programmer to build an AI agent?
faq.a: To build one from scratch, yes — you write the loop and the tool functions in a language like Python or TypeScript, usually with a provider SDK. But many no-code and low-code platforms now let you assemble agents by configuring tools and prompts visually, so you can start without writing the loop yourself.

faq.q: Are AI agents the same as RAG?
faq.a: No, but they overlap. RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) fetches relevant documents and feeds them to the model to ground its answer. An agent can use retrieval as one of its tools, but an agent is the broader loop-and-act pattern, while RAG is specifically about pulling in outside knowledge.

faq.q: Why are AI agents unreliable on long tasks?
faq.a: Errors compound. If each step is 95% reliable, a 10-step task succeeds only around 60% of the time, because one bad step can derail the rest. Long runs need guardrails — retries, validation after each step, step limits, and sometimes a human approval check — to stay dependable.

furtherReading.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models (arXiv)
